planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring or fall in moist, consistently irrigated soil; plants establish quickly and begin producing berries in their second or third season.

directions: Plant in moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to full shade; red-osier dogwood produces its most vivid winter stem color in full sun but tolerates significant shade, making it one of the few native shrubs that lights up a shady winter garden with brilliant crimson branches. The white berries ripen in summer and are eaten by over 40 bird species, and the dense thicket provides year-round nesting cover. Rejuvenate the shrub every two to three years by cutting one-third of the oldest canes to the ground in early spring, since the brightest stem color comes from young growth. The stoloniferous habit forms broad colonies excellent for streambank stabilization and erosion control.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall; very adaptable — tolerates clay, compaction, and a wide range of moisture levels once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist soil in full sun to moderate shade; gray dogwood is one of the most wildlife-friendly native shrubs in the eastern US, producing clusters of white berries on distinctive red pedicels in late summer that attract catbirds, robins, thrushes, and over 90 other bird species. The shrub tolerates dry soil, compacted clay, and partial shade better than most native dogwoods. It spreads by root suckers to form dense thickets valuable for erosion control and wildlife corridors; remove suckers in spring if a more contained form is desired. Small white flower clusters in early summer are visited by numerous native bees and short-tongued pollinators.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; avoid hot, dry exposures.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; running strawberry bush is a low-growing native groundcover that spreads by layering its trailing stems wherever they contact moist soil, creating dense weed-suppressing mats beneath trees. The plant is most ornamental in fall when the deeply lobed, strawberry-like orange-red fruits split open to reveal bright orange seeds — a striking display that wildlife relish. Unlike the invasive introduced burning bush (Euonymus alatus), this native species is well-behaved and restricted to shady, moist woodland habitats. Excellent for naturalizing dry to moist shaded slopes where little else will grow.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in fall or early spring; seeds need 2–3 months of cold stratification and should be sown outdoors in fall or cold-stratified in the refrigerator before spring sowing.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; beaked hazelnut is distinguished from American hazelnut (Corylus americana) by the long, tubular, beak-like husk that encloses each nut — the 'beak' can be twice the length of the nut itself. The nuts ripen in late summer and are enthusiastically gathered by squirrels, chipmunks, turkeys, woodpeckers, and grouse; you must act quickly to harvest any for yourself. Plant two or more seedlings for reliable cross-pollination since hazelnuts are wind-pollinated and often self-infertile. The catkins that appear in late winter before the leaves are an early source of pollen for native bees.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring before leafout; cuttings root readily if kept moist and placed in sand or moist potting mix. Direct seed is possible but seeds lose viability within days of ripening and must be sown immediately.

directions: Plant in well-drained to slightly moist, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to light shade; prairie willow is one of the few willows adapted to dry upland sites rather than wetlands, making it valuable for stabilizing dry slopes and prairie edges. The fuzzy silver catkins emerge in earliest spring before the leaves — earlier than most other willows — providing critical pollen and nectar for queen bumblebees and early-emerging native bees. Prairie willow is an important larval host plant for the viceroy butterfly and several species of giant silk moths. The plant forms colonies through root suckering and tolerates fire.
